1:
On 1 John 1:1 — “That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes… and our hands have handled, of the Word of life” — the witnesses mark the apostolic testimony to the incarnate Word.

The Latin fathers open with the incarnation. Augustine: who handles the Word with hands “except because the Word was made flesh and dwelt in us”? The grammatical-historical Robertson: the neuter relative (ho) points to the message “concerning the Word of life” (logou tes zoes), recalling John 1:1, John 1:14. Wesley: “from the beginning” — before he was manifested. The Reformed Calvin: life has been exhibited to us in Christ.

The verse opens with the apostles’ eyewitness testimony. The Word of life, from the beginning, was heard, seen, and handled. The eternal Christ truly entered human experience.

So the witnesses see John open his epistle, like his Gospel, with the eternal Word now made flesh and known by the senses. The witnesses note the solemn opening: “that which was from the beginning” — pointing back, like the prologue of the Gospel, to the Word who was in the beginning with God, eternal and pre-existent. They mark the accumulation of eyewitness verbs: “which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes… and our hands have handled” — the apostles testify not by hearsay or conjecture but by the fullest sensory certainty; they heard, gazed upon, and physically touched him. They observe Augustine’s decisive point: that hands could handle the Word means nothing less than that “the Word was made flesh” — the incarnation is the ground of the whole testimony, against any who would make Christ a phantom. They note Robertson’s grammatical observation that the neuter “that which” embraces the whole message concerning the Word of life, both the person and the gospel about him. They observe that “the Word of life” joins the two great titles: he is the Word (as in the Gospel) and the Life. They note that this emphatic testimony answers the early errorists who denied Christ’s true humanity. The verse thus founds the epistle on apostolic eyewitness: the eternal Word of life, from the beginning, was truly heard, seen, and handled — a manifestation the next verse declares.
2:
On 1 John 1:2 — “For the life was manifested, and we have seen it, and bear witness, and shew unto you that eternal life, which was with the Father, and was manifested unto us” — the witnesses mark the manifestation of the eternal life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parenthesis. Robertson: “was manifested” (ephanerothe), to make known what already exists; verse 2 is a parenthesis. Adam Clarke: the Lord Jesus, the fountain of life, manifested in the flesh. The Reformed Calvin: Christ, who is life and the fountain of life, has been manifested. Wesley: the living Word manifested in the flesh to our very senses.

The verse expands on the Word of life. The eternal life, which was with the Father, was manifested and witnessed. What was hidden in God appeared to men.

So the witnesses see John pause to unfold the great theme of “the Word of life”: the manifesting of eternal life. The witnesses note that this verse is a parenthesis, explaining the phrase just used. They mark the central declaration: “the life was manifested” — the Life, who is Christ himself, the fountain and giver of life, was made openly known; what the word “manifested” implies, as Robertson observes, is the making visible of what already existed, eternal and unseen. They observe the apostolic response: “we have seen it, and bear witness, and shew unto you” — having seen the manifested Life, the apostles testify and proclaim it; sight grounds witness, and witness issues in preaching. They note the profound description of what was manifested: “that eternal life, which was with the Father” — the Life that from eternity dwelt with the Father, in intimate communion, now made known to men; this is the very language of personal pre-existence and fellowship within the Godhead. They observe Calvin’s point that Christ, who is life itself and the fountain of life, has been exhibited, that our affections might be kindled toward this incomparable good. They note the movement: the eternal, hidden Life has come forth from the Father into open manifestation. The verse thus deepens the opening: the eternal life that was with the Father has been manifested, seen, and proclaimed — the very gospel John declares that his readers may share its fellowship.
3:
On 1 John 1:3 — “That which we have seen and heard declare we unto you, that ye also may have fellowship with us: and truly our fellowship is with the Father, and with his Son Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the goal of the testimony: fellowship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the purpose. Robertson: the third use of “we have seen”; emphasis by repetition. Adam Clarke: nothing by hearsay; that ye may be preserved from false doctrine. The Reformed Calvin: the heralds chosen by Christ were fit and faithful witnesses. Wesley: that ye may enjoy the same fellowship, with the Father and Son.

The verse states why John writes. The apostles declare what they saw and heard, that the readers may share their fellowship — fellowship with the Father and the Son. Testimony aims at communion.

So the witnesses see John state the purpose of his eyewitness proclamation: to bring his readers into fellowship. The witnesses note the repeated emphasis: “that which we have seen and heard” — the third such assertion, hammering home the certainty of the apostolic testimony; nothing here rests on rumor or guess. They mark the aim: “declare we unto you, that ye also may have fellowship with us” — the apostles preach the manifested Word so that their hearers may be joined in the same communion they enjoy; the gospel gathers believers into a shared partnership. They observe the height to which this fellowship rises: “and truly our fellowship is with the Father, and with his Son Jesus Christ” — the communion is not merely human and horizontal, but vertical and divine; to be in fellowship with the apostles is to be brought into fellowship with God himself, Father and Son. They note that this is the astonishing privilege of the gospel: through the testimony to Christ, believers are admitted to communion with the very God. They observe Calvin’s point that the apostles were chosen as faithful witnesses precisely to convey this. They note that fellowship, not mere information, is the goal. The verse thus declares the purpose of the epistle: the apostolic witness aims to draw the readers into fellowship with the apostles, and through them with the Father and the Son — a fellowship whose fruit, the next verse adds, is fullness of joy.
4:
On 1 John 1:4 — “And these things write we unto you, that your joy may be full” — the witnesses mark the aim of fullness of joy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the echo. Robertson: “may be fulfilled” (pepleromene), stressing the state of completion; cf. John 15:11; John 16:24. Adam Clarke: that your happiness may be complete. The Reformed Calvin: full joy expresses the complete happiness obtained through the Gospel. Wesley: the joy of faith here directly intended.

The verse adds a second purpose. John writes that the readers’ joy may be full. Fellowship with God overflows in complete joy.

So the witnesses see John name a further purpose of his writing: the fullness of his readers’ joy. The witnesses note the close echo of Christ’s own words to the disciples, that his joy might remain in them and their joy be full; John, the beloved disciple, takes up his Master’s language. They mark the aim: “that your joy may be full” — not a partial or fleeting gladness, but a complete, brimming joy; the perfect tense, as Robertson notes, stresses the settled state of fullness. They observe that this joy flows directly from the fellowship just described: to be in communion with the Father and the Son is the spring of the deepest and most complete happiness; Calvin says full joy expresses the perfect blessedness obtained through the gospel. They note Wesley’s point that the joy of faith is here intended — the gladness that springs from believing and from the fellowship faith brings. They observe that John writes not to burden but to gladden; the truths he sets down are meant to complete his readers’ joy. They note that this joins joy to fellowship: the communion with God that the gospel brings is the only ground of a joy that is full. The verse thus states the warm aim of the epistle: that the readers, brought into fellowship with God, may have their joy made full — before John turns to the first great message, that God is light.

So the witnesses see John name a further purpose: the fullness of his readers’ joy. The verse closely echoes Christ’s own words to the disciples, that his joy might remain in them and theirs be full; John, the beloved disciple, takes up his Master’s language. The aim is “that your joy may be full” — not a partial or fleeting gladness but a complete, brimming joy, the perfect tense stressing the settled state. This joy flows directly from the fellowship just described: to be in communion with the Father and the Son is the spring of the deepest happiness (Calvin: the perfect blessedness through the gospel). John writes not to burden but to gladden; the truths he sets down are meant to complete his readers’ joy.
5:
On 1 John 1:5 — “This then is the message… that God is light, and in him is no darkness at all” — the witnesses mark the great declaration of God’s holiness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the message. Robertson: “message” (angelia), only here and 1 John 3:11; from God (ap’ autou). The Puritan Henry: a report from the author of the gospel for our conviction. Wesley: “God is light — the light of wisdom, love, holiness, glory… no contrary principle.” The Reformed Calvin prefers “annunciation.”

The verse gives the heart of the message. God is light, with no darkness in him. His perfect holiness is the standard of fellowship.

So the witnesses see John state the central message received from Christ: God is light. The witnesses note the solemnity: “this then is the message which we have heard of him” — the very announcement that comes from Christ himself, the sum of the revelation. They mark the great declaration: “God is light” — a comprehensive figure; as Wesley unfolds it, light stands for God’s wisdom, holiness, purity, glory, and love — what light is to the natural eye, God is to the spiritual. They observe the absolute negation: “and in him is no darkness at all” — no admixture of ignorance, sin, error, or evil; God is perfectly pure, with no contrary principle whatever. They note that this declaration is the foundation for the whole moral argument that follows: because God is light, those who would have fellowship with him must walk in light, not in the darkness of sin. They observe that “light” speaks at once of holiness and of self-revealing glory: God is pure, and God makes himself known. They note that this message, drawn from Christ, sets the standard against which all professed fellowship will be tested. The verse thus lays down the great premise of the epistle: God is light, with no darkness at all — the holiness that, the next verses show, exposes the lie of those who claim fellowship yet walk in darkness.

So the witnesses see John state the central message received from Christ: God is light. The solemnity: “this then is the message which we have heard of him” — the very announcement from Christ himself, the sum of the revelation. The great declaration “God is light” is a comprehensive figure: as Wesley unfolds it, light stands for God’s wisdom, holiness, purity, glory, and love — what light is to the natural eye, God is to the spiritual. The absolute negation “in him is no darkness at all” admits no ignorance, sin, error, or evil, no contrary principle. This is the foundation for the whole moral argument: because God is light, those who would have fellowship with him must walk in light, not in the darkness of sin.
6:
On 1 John 1:6 — “If we say that we have fellowship with him, and walk in darkness, we lie, and do not the truth” — the witnesses mark the first test of false profession.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contradiction. Robertson: claiming fellowship (verse 3) involves walking in light, not darkness. Adam Clarke: to profess communion and walk in darkness — live a sinful life — is to lie. The Reformed Calvin: an argument from what is inconsistent. Wesley: if we say it with tongue or heart, while walking in sin, we do not the truth.

The verse exposes the first false claim. To claim fellowship with God while walking in darkness is a lie. Profession without holiness is false.

So the witnesses see John apply the great premise — God is light — to test the first false profession. The witnesses note the claim: “if we say that we have fellowship with him” — if anyone professes communion with God, to be partaker of his nature and to enjoy his presence. They mark the contradicting reality: “and walk in darkness” — yet lives in sin, in the moral darkness opposed to God’s light; the manner of life flatly contradicts the profession. They observe the verdict: “we lie, and do not the truth” — such a one lies, both in word and in deed; the profession is a falsehood, and the life proves it; he neither speaks nor practices the truth. They note Calvin’s logic of inconsistency: since God is light and sanctifies all who are his, to claim fellowship with the holy God while walking in darkness is a plain self-contradiction. They observe that John strikes here at any religion of mere profession divorced from holy living — perhaps the early errorists who claimed high knowledge of God while indulging sin. They note that fellowship with the God who is light cannot coexist with a life in darkness. The verse thus exposes the first lie: to profess fellowship with God while walking in darkness is to lie and not do the truth — against which the next verse sets the reality of walking in light.
7:
On 1 John 1:7 — “But if we walk in the light, as he is in the light, we have fellowship one with another, and the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin” — the witnesses mark the reality of walking in light and the cleansing blood.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel. Robertson: “as he” — God is light (v. 5) and dwells in unapproachable light (1 Timothy 6:16). Barnes: walking in light means holiness, truth, and openness. Adam Clarke: having received holiness from him, we have communion with God. The blood cleanseth from all sin.

The verse states the true case. Walking in the light brings fellowship and cleansing by Christ’s blood. Holiness and atonement go together.

So the witnesses see John set the true reality against the false claim: walking in light brings fellowship and cleansing. The witnesses note the condition: “if we walk in the light, as he is in the light” — if our lives are characterized by holiness, truth, and openness, conformed to the God who himself is light and dwells in light; the believer’s walk answers to God’s own nature. They mark the first fruit: “we have fellowship one with another” — communion both with God and among believers; those who walk in the light are joined in true fellowship, the very thing the false professor only claimed. They observe the second, precious fruit: “and the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin” — even as they walk in the light, believers are continually cleansed by the atoning blood of Christ from all their sin; the present tense marks an ongoing purification. They note the balance the verse holds: walking in the light is required, yet even the holiest still needs the cleansing blood; light and atonement are not opposed but joined. They observe that this guards against both the lawless professor (who ignores holiness) and the perfectionist (who imagines no further need of cleansing). They note the comprehensiveness: “from all sin.” The verse thus sets forth the gospel reality: those who walk in the light have fellowship and are cleansed from all sin by Christ’s blood — which is why, the next verse insists, none may claim to be without sin.
8:
On 1 John 1:8 — “If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us” — the witnesses mark the warning against denying sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the error. Robertson: “we have no sin” — no principle of sin; some Gnostics held this, matter being evil but the soul untainted. The Puritan Henry: he justifies the supposition that even saints have sin. Adam Clarke: all have sinned and need a Saviour. The Reformed Calvin: he commends grace from its necessity.

The verse condemns the denial of sin. To say we have no sin is self-deception. Even believers must own their sinfulness.

So the witnesses see John turn from the false claim of sinless fellowship to the false claim of sinlessness itself. The witnesses note the error: “if we say that we have no sin” — if anyone professes to have no sin, no inward principle or taint of it, to be already perfect and pure. They mark that this was a real error, perhaps among the early Gnostics, who (as Robertson notes) held that matter was evil but the spiritual soul untainted, and so denied personal guilt. They observe the verdict: “we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us” — such a one is self-deceived, fooling no one but himself; and the truth, the saving reality of the gospel, has no place in him. They note Henry’s point that John, having shown that even those in heavenly fellowship still have sin (v. 7 implies it), now defends that supposition by exposing the danger of denying it. They observe Calvin’s sense that John commends grace from its necessity: since no one is free from sin, all need the remedy of pardon; to deny sin is to spurn the grace that saves. They note that this guards the cleansing blood of verse 7: precisely because believers still sin, they need that continual cleansing. The verse thus warns against the proud denial of sin: to claim sinlessness is to deceive oneself and to be void of the truth — whereas, the next verse promises, confession finds forgiveness.
9:
On 1 John 1:9 — “If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness” — the witnesses mark the promise to confession.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the promise. Robertson: “if we keep on confessing” (homologomen, present); confession urged throughout the N.T. (James 5:16). Adam Clarke: from a deep sense of guilt, imploring mercy. The Reformed Calvin: a reconciliation ready for us when we sin. Wesley: faithful, because he promised; just — and so he will pardon.

The verse gives the great promise. Confessing our sins, God faithfully and justly forgives and cleanses. Honest confession meets sure pardon.

So the witnesses see John set against the denial of sin the gracious way of confession and forgiveness. The witnesses note the condition: “if we confess our sins” — the present tense, as Robertson notes, marking a continual, habitual confession; the believer, from a deep sense of guilt, openly acknowledges his sins to God, not hiding or excusing them. They mark the ground of the promise in God’s own character: “he is faithful and just” — faithful, because he has promised forgiveness and will keep his word; and, strikingly, just — not merely merciful but righteous in forgiving. They observe the wonder Wesley and others note in “just”: one might expect that justice would punish, yet here God’s very justice secures pardon — because the blood of Christ has satisfied justice, God is righteous in forgiving the confessing sinner; to forgive is now the just thing to do. They note the twofold blessing: “to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness” — both the pardon of guilt and the purging of the pollution; God deals with sin completely. They observe Calvin’s comfort that a reconciliation stands ready and prepared for the believer who sins. They note that confession, not the denial of verse 8, is the way to peace. The verse thus holds out the gospel’s assurance: confessing sin, the believer finds God faithful and just to forgive and cleanse — for, as the next verse adds, to deny sin makes God a liar.
10:
On 1 John 1:10 — “If we say that we have not sinned, we make him a liar, and his word is not in us” — the witnesses mark the gravest form of the denial.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the distinction. Robertson: “we have not sinned” (perfect of hamartano) — a denial of specific acts of sin, while verse 8 denied the principle. Barnes: to claim a wholly upright past makes God untrue. The Reformed Calvin: they who claim purity blaspheme God, who declares all guilty.

The verse names the worst denial. To say we have not sinned makes God a liar. Such a claim contradicts God’s own word.

So the witnesses see John drive the warning to its sharpest point: to deny having sinned makes God himself a liar. The witnesses note the distinction the expositors draw between this and verse 8: there the denial was of the principle or presence of sin (“we have no sin”); here it is the denial of actual sins committed (“we have not sinned”), a claim to a wholly innocent past. They mark the appalling consequence: “we make him a liar” — since God throughout Scripture declares the whole human race guilty of sin, the one who claims never to have sinned charges God with falsehood, contradicting his clear testimony. They observe Calvin’s strong language that such a person blasphemes God and carries on war with him, accusing him of untruth. They note the further loss: “and his word is not in us” — the word of God, which everywhere convicts of sin, has found no place in such a heart; its absence is proved by the denial. They observe that this is the climax of the section: the proud denial of sin not only deceives oneself (v. 8) but impugns the truthfulness of God. They note the contrast with the humble confession of verse 9, which honors God’s word and finds forgiveness. The verse thus seals the warning: to claim sinlessness is to make God a liar and to be void of his word — the sobering ground on which John, turning to comfort, points to the Advocate.
